data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was caused by a military-social revolution where mobile infantry armed with javelins and swords, recruited from the margins of palatial society, overthrew the chariot-based aristocracies in a synchronized wave of internal rebellion, not external invasion.", "mechanism": "Palace elites monopolized expensive chariots and bronze armor, creating a rigid social hierarchy. Population growth and land pressure produced a disenfranchised class of 'runners' (the Shardana, Lukka, etc. of Egyptian texts). These groups, using terrain and swarm tactics, first raided and then systematically dismantled the palaces from within, burning administrative centers to erase debt and obligation records, which explains the targeted destruction of archives.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Destruction layers uniformly show evidence of siege engines and organized external armies rather than rapid, targeted burning of administrative centers", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Linear B and other texts from the final years record a strengthening of the palace system and loyalty to the wanax/king, not social unrest", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Egypt survived because its pharaonic state had already integrated 'Libyan' and other mercenary groups into its military structure, co-opting the revolutionary energy, and its grain-based wealth could pay off potential rebels."}
